expanded_description: This section explores two of the most important plant hormones—auxins and gibberellins—that regulate virtually every aspect of plant growth and development. You will learn how these chemical messengers are produced, transported, and perceived by plant cells to coordinate processes ranging from cell elongation and division to flowering and fruit development. By understanding auxin and gibberellin function, you will appreciate how plants integrate environmental signals with internal developmental programs, how farmers and gardeners manipulate these hormones to improve crop yields, and how hormonal balance determines plant form and function throughout the life cycle.

core_concept.principle: Plant hormones (phytohormones) are organic compounds produced in small quantities that regulate growth and development by coordinating cellular responses to environmental and developmental signals, with auxins primarily controlling cell elongation and polarity while gibberellins primarily stimulate stem elongation and seed germination.
core_concept.explanation: Unlike animals, plants lack a nervous system and must rely on chemical signaling to coordinate growth across their entire body. Plant hormones are produced in specific tissues, transported to target cells, and perceived by receptors that trigger signal transduction cascades leading to changes in gene expression and cellular behavior. Auxins (primarily indole-3-acetic acid, IAA) are produced in shoot apical meristems and young leaves, transported polarly (basipetally) through specialized transport proteins, and regulate cell elongation, apical dominance, root initiation, and tropic responses. Gibberellins (GAs, with GA₁ and GA₃ being most active) are produced in young tissues and developing seeds, transported non-polarly through phloem and xylem, and stimulate stem elongation, seed germination, flowering, and fruit development. These hormones don't act in isolation—they interact with each other and with other hormones (cytokinins, abscisic acid, ethylene) to create complex regulatory networks that determine plant form and function. Understanding these hormones reveals how plants achieve coordinated growth without a central nervous system, how environmental factors influence development, and how agricultural practices can manipulate hormone levels to improve crop performance.

image.alt: Diagram showing auxin production in shoot tip with polar transport downward, gibberellin production in young tissues with non-polar transport, and their respective effects on cell elongation, root formation, stem growth, and seed germination
image.caption: Plant hormone distribution and function: auxins (blue arrows) produced in shoot tips regulate cell elongation and apical dominance; gibberellins (green arrows) produced in young tissues stimulate stem elongation and seed germination.

sections.section_title: Auxins and Gibberellins: Chemical Messengers of Plant Growth
sections.introduction: Plant hormones represent one of nature's most elegant solutions to the challenge of coordinating growth in organisms that cannot move. While animals use nervous systems for rapid communication, plants rely on chemical messengers that travel through tissues to synchronize development across the entire organism. Auxins and gibberellins were among the first plant hormones discovered—auxins in the 1920s through experiments on phototropism, gibberellins in the 1930s from research on the 'foolish seedling' disease in rice. Today, we understand that these hormones function as master regulators of plant development, integrating environmental cues (light, gravity, temperature) with internal developmental programs to determine when and where cells divide, elongate, and differentiate. This section will guide you through the biosynthesis, transport, perception, and function of auxins and gibberellins, revealing how these molecules orchestrate the remarkable plasticity of plant growth and form.

key_concept.definition: Auxins are a class of plant hormones primarily represented by indole-3-acetic acid (IAA) that regulate cell elongation, apical dominance, root initiation, and tropic responses through polar transport and concentration-dependent effects; gibberellins are diterpenoid acids (primarily GA₁ and GA₃) that stimulate stem elongation, seed germination, flowering, and fruit development through non-polar transport and signaling pathways.
key_concept.context: Plant hormones function at extremely low concentrations (10⁻⁶ to 10⁻⁹ M), with effects determined by concentration, location, developmental stage, and interactions with other hormones. Unlike animal hormones produced in specialized glands, plant hormones are synthesized in multiple tissues and act locally or at a distance. The same hormone can have different effects in different tissues—auxin promotes shoot growth but inhibits root growth at the same concentration. This context-dependency creates complex regulatory networks that enable plants to adapt growth to environmental conditions.

image.alt: Graph showing auxin concentration effects on shoot vs. root growth with optimal concentrations marked for each tissue type
image.caption: Hormone concentration effects: auxin promotes shoot growth at moderate concentrations (10⁻⁵ M) but inhibits root growth at the same concentration; roots require much lower concentrations (10⁻¹⁰ M) for optimal growth.

classifications.value: Used for classifying the production and movement of auxins within plants, primarily synthesized from tryptophan in shoot apical meristems and young leaves, transported polarly (basipetally) through specialized transport proteins.
characteristics: Primary auxin: indole-3-acetic acid (IAA), synthesized from tryptophan via multiple pathways
Production sites: shoot apical meristem, young leaves, developing seeds, root tips
Polar transport: basipetal (shoot to root) via PIN proteins and AUX/LAX influx carriers
Transport rate: ~1 cm/hour, much slower than phloem transport
Concentration gradient: highest at shoot tip, decreasing toward base
Transport requires energy (ATP) and is sensitive to environmental factors
classifications.behavior: Auxin biosynthesis occurs primarily in shoot tips where tryptophan is converted to IAA through several enzymatic pathways. Once synthesized, auxin moves cell-to-cell through polar transport: it enters cells via AUX/LAX influx carriers, accumulates in cytoplasm (weak acid trap), and exits via PIN efflux carriers positioned asymmetrically on cell membranes. This asymmetric PIN positioning creates directional flow from shoot to root. Polar transport enables auxin to establish concentration gradients that pattern plant development—high auxin at shoot tip maintains apical dominance; lateral auxin maxima initiate leaf and root primordia; auxin redistribution mediates tropic responses. Transport can be redirected by environmental cues: gravity repositions PIN proteins to redirect auxin flow during gravitropism; light asymmetry redistributes auxin during phototropism.

examples.explanation: The shoot apical meristem produces auxin that flows downward through the stem, inhibiting lateral bud growth. This maintains a single dominant shoot, optimizing light capture. If the apical meristem is removed (pruning), auxin source is eliminated, lateral buds grow, creating bushier growth. Gardeners exploit this by pruning to shape plants. The mechanism involves auxin inducing ethylene production in buds, which inhibits bud outgrowth, and auxin regulating cytokinin distribution that promotes bud growth.

image.alt: Diagram showing auxin flow from shoot tip down stem inhibiting lateral buds, with pruning removing auxin source allowing bud growth
image.caption: Apical dominance: auxin from shoot tip (blue arrows) flows downward, suppressing lateral buds; pruning removes auxin source, allowing buds to grow.

examples.explanation: When stem cuttings are placed in water or soil, auxin accumulates at the cut base, stimulating root primordia formation. Commercial rooting powders contain synthetic auxins (NAA, IBA) that enhance this process. The mechanism involves auxin inducing cell division in pericycle cells, forming root primordia that emerge through the stem. This property enables vegetative propagation of many plant species, crucial for horticulture and agriculture.

When light shines from one side, auxin redistributes to the shaded side of the shoot. Higher auxin concentration on the shaded side stimulates cell elongation, causing the shoot to bend toward light. This maximizes light capture for photosynthesis. The redistribution occurs within minutes via PIN protein repositioning, demonstrating rapid hormonal response to environmental cues.

classifications.value: Used for classifying the production and movement of gibberellins within plants, synthesized from acetyl-CoA via the terpenoid pathway in young tissues and developing seeds, transported non-polarly through phloem and xylem.
characteristics: Over 130 gibberellins identified; GA₁, GA₃, GA₄, GA₇ most biologically active
Biosynthesis: from acetyl-CoA via mevalonic acid pathway in plastids, ER, and cytosol
Production sites: young leaves, shoot apices, root tips, developing seeds
Non-polar transport: moves bidirectionally through phloem and xylem
Transport rate: faster than auxin, moves with photosynthate in phloem
Active GAs synthesized from inactive precursors via hydroxylation
classifications.behavior: Gibberellin biosynthesis occurs in multiple steps across different cellular compartments: early steps in plastids, intermediate steps in endoplasmic reticulum, final activation in cytosol. Inactive GA precursors (e.g., GA₁₂) are converted to active forms (GA₁, GA₄) by hydroxylase enzymes. This two-step synthesis enables regulation at multiple points. Unlike auxin's polar transport, gibberellins move non-polarly through vascular tissue, enabling long-distance signaling from production sites to target tissues. Developing seeds are major GA sources, producing GAs that stimulate fruit growth and mobilize seed reserves during germination. GA levels are regulated by biosynthesis enzymes (GA3ox activates, GA2ox deactivates) and environmental factors: light promotes GA biosynthesis in germinating seeds; cold stratification increases GA sensitivity; shade increases GA levels to promote stem elongation.

examples.explanation: Many plants (lettuce, cabbage) grow as rosettes (short stems with leaves radiating from base) until flowering. Gibberellins stimulate internode elongation, converting rosettes to bolted stems with flowers. This 'bolting' is triggered by environmental cues (day length, temperature) that increase GA biosynthesis. Commercial lettuce growers apply GA to induce uniform bolting for seed production. The mechanism involves GA promoting cell elongation and division in stem internodes via cell wall loosening enzymes (expansins) and cell cycle regulators.

image.alt: Comparison of lettuce rosette (left) vs. bolted stem after GA treatment (right) showing dramatic stem elongation
image.caption: Gibberellin-induced bolting: rosette plants (left) elongate stems (right) when GA levels increase, enabling flowering and seed production.

examples.explanation: During seed germination, the embryo produces GA that diffuses to the aleurone layer (outer endosperm). GA induces aleurone cells to produce α-amylase and other hydrolytic enzymes that break down stored starch into sugars. These sugars fuel embryo growth until photosynthesis begins. This mechanism enables seeds to mobilize reserves precisely when needed. Barley brewers exploit this by applying GA to malt barley, stimulating enzyme production for starch conversion to fermentable sugars.

Gibberellins stimulate cell division and elongation in developing fruits. Commercial grape growers apply GA to increase berry size and produce seedless varieties. GA treatment can increase grape cluster size by 50-100%, demonstrating the hormone's commercial importance. The mechanism involves GA promoting cell wall expansion and cell division in fruit tissues, coordinated with auxin and cytokinin signals.

classifications.value: Used for classifying the molecular mechanism by which auxin perception triggers cellular responses, involving TIR1/AFB receptors, Aux/IAA repressors, and ARF transcription factors.
characteristics: Receptors: TIR1/AFB family of F-box proteins in nucleus
Repressors: Aux/IAA proteins that inhibit ARF transcription factors
Activators: ARF (Auxin Response Factor) transcription factors
Mechanism: auxin binds TIR1, promoting Aux/IAA degradation via ubiquitin-proteasome pathway
Response time: minutes to hours for gene expression changes
Specificity: different ARFs activate different gene sets in different tissues
classifications.behavior: In the absence of auxin, Aux/IAA repressor proteins bind ARF transcription factors, preventing them from activating auxin-responsive genes. When auxin enters the nucleus, it binds to TIR1 receptors, which act as co-receptors. The auxin-TIR1 complex binds Aux/IAA proteins, targeting them for ubiquitination and degradation by the proteasome. With repressors removed, ARFs activate transcription of auxin-responsive genes (SAUR, GH3, Aux/IAA themselves—creating feedback loops). Different ARFs are expressed in different tissues, creating tissue-specific responses: ARF5/MP promotes vascular development; ARF7/19 mediate phototropism; ARF10/16 regulate root development. This modular system enables one hormone to produce diverse effects across the plant. The pathway is rapidly reversible: when auxin levels drop, new Aux/IAA proteins accumulate, repressing ARFs and shutting down responses.

examples.explanation: Auxin stimulates plasma membrane H⁺-ATPases that pump protons into cell walls, lowering pH. Acidic pH activates expansins—proteins that loosen cell wall cross-links. With walls loosened, turgor pressure drives cell expansion. This 'acid growth hypothesis' explains rapid auxin-induced elongation (minutes). Gene expression changes (hours) sustain elongation by producing new wall materials. This two-phase response enables both rapid and sustained growth responses.

image.alt: Diagram showing auxin activating H⁺-ATPase, acidifying cell wall, activating expansins, and enabling cell expansion
image.caption: Acid growth mechanism: auxin activates proton pumps (1), acidifying cell wall (2), activating expansins (3), enabling cell expansion (4).

examples.explanation: Auxin accumulates at specific pericycle cells, activating ARF7/19 that induce cell division genes. Dividing cells form lateral root primordia that emerge through the cortex and epidermis. This process creates the branched root system that maximizes soil exploration. Auxin maxima are established by polar transport redirecting auxin flow to specific sites, demonstrating how transport patterns create developmental patterns.

Auxin flow through stems induces vascular tissue (xylem and phloem) differentiation along the flow path. This creates a positive feedback: vascular tissue enables auxin transport, which induces more vascular tissue. When stems are wounded, auxin accumulates at wound sites, inducing vascular regeneration that reconnects transport pathways. This property enables grafting—joining plant parts that heal through auxin-induced vascular reconnection.

classifications.value: Used for classifying the molecular mechanism by which gibberellin perception triggers cellular responses, involving GID1 receptors, DELLA repressors, and transcriptional regulation.
characteristics: Receptors: GID1 (Gibberellin Insensitive Dwarf1) soluble receptors in nucleus and cytoplasm
Repressors: DELLA proteins that inhibit growth-promoting transcription factors
Mechanism: GA binds GID1, promoting DELLA degradation via ubiquitin-proteasome pathway
Response time: hours to days for growth responses
Specificity: different DELLA proteins regulate different processes
Integration: DELLA proteins interact with multiple hormone pathways (auxin, ABA, ethylene)
classifications.behavior: DELLA proteins are growth repressors that inhibit transcription factors promoting cell elongation and division. In the absence of GA, DELLAs accumulate and suppress growth. When GA enters cells, it binds GID1 receptors. The GA-GID1 complex binds DELLAs, targeting them for ubiquitination and proteasomal degradation. With DELLAs removed, growth-promoting transcription factors (PIFs, BZR1) activate genes for cell wall loosening, cell cycle progression, and metabolic enzymes. Different DELLA proteins regulate different processes: RGA regulates stem elongation; GAI regulates seed germination; RGL2 regulates flower development. DELLAs also integrate multiple hormone signals: they interact with auxin signaling components, ABA signaling (promoting dormancy), and ethylene signaling. This integration enables coordinated responses to multiple environmental cues. The pathway is tunable: GA levels, GID1 expression, and DELLA stability all regulate response magnitude.

examples.explanation: In dormant seeds, DELLAs accumulate, suppressing germination genes. When conditions favor germination (water, light, temperature), GA biosynthesis increases. GA binds GID1, degrading DELLAs. This releases suppression of α-amylase and other germination genes. ABA (abscisic acid) opposes GA by stabilizing DELLAs, maintaining dormancy. The GA/ABA balance determines germination timing, ensuring seeds germinate only when conditions support seedling survival.

image.alt: Diagram showing GA binding GID1, degrading DELLA repressors, and releasing germination gene expression
image.caption: GA signaling in germination: GA binds GID1 receptor (1), DELLA degradation (2), germination genes activated (3), seed germinates (4).

examples.explanation: When plants detect shade (low red:far-red light ratio), they increase GA biosynthesis and decrease DELLA stability. This promotes stem elongation to reach light. The response involves phytochrome photoreceptors regulating GA metabolism genes. Plants in dense canopies grow taller but thinner, sacrificing strength for height. This plasticity enables competition for light but increases lodging (falling over) risk in crops.

High-yielding wheat and rice varieties from the Green Revolution carry mutations in GA signaling genes. Wheat Rht genes encode DELLA proteins resistant to GA-induced degradation; rice sd1 gene reduces GA biosynthesis. These mutations reduce stem height, preventing lodging while maintaining grain yield. Shorter stems allocate more resources to grains rather than straw. This demonstrates how understanding hormone pathways enables crop improvement for food security.

principles.statement: Plant hormone effects depend on concentration gradients established by localized biosynthesis and directional transport, with different concentrations triggering different developmental outcomes in different tissues.
principles.application: This principle explains how one hormone produces diverse effects: auxin at 10⁻⁵ M promotes shoot elongation but inhibits root elongation; at 10⁻¹⁰ M it promotes root elongation. Understanding gradients enables manipulation: applying auxin to cuttings induces roots; applying to fruits prevents drop. In agriculture, synthetic hormones are applied at specific concentrations to achieve desired outcomes without unwanted side effects.
principles.example: In root gravitropism, gravity causes auxin to accumulate on the lower side of the root. At high concentration, auxin inhibits cell elongation on the lower side while the upper side continues elongating, causing the root to bend downward. The same auxin concentration that promotes shoot elongation inhibits root elongation, demonstrating tissue-specific concentration responses.
principles.analogy: Hormone gradients are like volume controls: the same song (hormone) sounds different at different volumes (concentrations). Low volume (low concentration) might be soothing (root growth); high volume (high concentration) might be overwhelming (root inhibition). Different rooms (tissues) have different optimal volumes.

image.alt: Diagram showing auxin concentration gradient from shoot tip to root with different effects at different concentrations along the gradient
image.caption: Hormone gradients: auxin concentration decreases from shoot tip (high) to root (low), with different concentrations triggering different developmental responses in different tissues.

principles.statement: Plant hormones don't act in isolation but interact through synergistic, antagonistic, and additive relationships, creating regulatory networks that integrate multiple signals for coordinated growth responses.
principles.application: This principle explains why single-hormone treatments sometimes fail: outcomes depend on the hormonal context. Auxin and cytokinin ratio determines root vs. shoot formation in tissue culture (high auxin = roots; high cytokinin = shoots). GA and ABA antagonism controls seed germination (GA promotes, ABA inhibits). Understanding interactions enables precise manipulation: applying cytokinin with auxin balances root/shoot growth; reducing ABA sensitivity enhances germination under stress.
principles.example: Apical dominance involves auxin-cytokinin-ethylene crosstalk: auxin from shoot tip inhibits lateral buds directly and indirectly by promoting ethylene (inhibitory) and reducing cytokinin (promoting) in buds. Removing the shoot tip reverses these effects, allowing bud growth. This network ensures robust control—multiple pathways prevent accidental bud activation.
principles.analogy: Hormone interactions are like an orchestra: each hormone is an instrument; the conductor (developmental program) coordinates them. A violin alone (one hormone) makes limited music; the full orchestra (hormone network) creates symphonies (coordinated growth). Changing one instrument's volume affects the whole performance.

principles.statement: Environmental factors (light, temperature, water, nutrients) regulate hormone biosynthesis, transport, and sensitivity, enabling plants to adjust growth to prevailing conditions.
principles.application: This principle explains plant plasticity: the same genotype produces different phenotypes in different environments via hormonal regulation. Shade increases GA (promotes elongation); drought increases ABA (reduces growth); cold increases GA sensitivity (promotes germination after winter). Agriculture exploits this: greenhouse lighting controls flowering; irrigation affects ABA levels; fertilization affects cytokinin production. Understanding environmental regulation enables optimization of growing conditions.
principles.example: Photoperiod controls flowering via hormone regulation: long days increase GA biosynthesis in long-day plants, promoting flowering. Short days increase ABA in short-day plants, also promoting flowering through different pathways. This ensures plants flower at optimal seasons. Greenhouse growers manipulate day length to control flowering time for market timing.
principles.analogy: Environmental regulation is like a thermostat: environment (temperature) regulates hormone production (heating/cooling), which adjusts growth (room temperature). The system maintains optimal conditions despite external changes. Different plants have different 'thermostat settings' adapted to their native environments.

components.definition: Auxin and gibberellin signaling involves integrated components: hormone biosynthesis enzymes, transport proteins, receptors, signal transduction cascades, and transcriptional regulators that together convert hormone perception into growth responses.
components.interpretation: This conceptual equation shows that growth responses depend on hormone concentration, receptor abundance and sensitivity, signal amplification in transduction cascades, repressor protein levels, and hormone degradation rates. Each component is regulatable: biosynthesis controls [Hormone]; gene expression controls Receptor sensitivity; phosphorylation controls Signal amplification; ubiquitination controls Reppressor levels and Degradation rate. This multi-point regulation enables fine-tuning of responses. Agricultural applications target specific components: synthetic auxins mimic hormone; GA biosynthesis inhibitors (paclobutrazol) reduce [Hormone]; DELLA mutations alter Reppressor levels.

image.alt: Integrated diagram showing auxin and gibberellin signaling pathways from biosynthesis through transport, reception, transduction, to growth responses
image.caption: Hormone signaling components: biosynthesis (production), transport (distribution), reception (perception), transduction (amplification), and response (growth) work together to regulate plant development.

worked_examples.title: Calculating Auxin Concentration for Root Induction
worked_examples.problem: A horticulturist wants to induce root formation on stem cuttings. Research shows optimal rooting occurs at 10⁻⁵ M auxin, with inhibition above 10⁻⁴ M and no effect below 10⁻⁶ M. If the stock solution is 10⁻² M IAA, calculate: (1) the dilution factor needed to achieve 10⁻⁵ M, (2) volume of stock needed to make 100 mL of working solution, and (3) explain why concentrations outside the optimal range fail. Show calculations.

step_by_step.explanation: Calculate dilution factor: divide stock concentration by target concentration.
Calculate volume of stock needed for 100 mL working solution using C₁V₁ = C₂V₂.
Calculate volume of diluent (water or buffer) needed.
Explain concentration effects: below 10⁻⁶ M, insufficient receptor occupancy; above 10⁻⁴ M, inhibitory pathways activated.

worked_examples.final_answer: (1) Dilution factor: 1000-fold. (2) Stock volume: 0.1 mL. (3) Diluent volume: 99.9 mL. Concentrations below 10⁻⁶ M fail because insufficient auxin receptors are occupied to trigger responses; concentrations above 10⁻⁴ M activate inhibitory pathways (ethylene production, growth suppression). This demonstrates the narrow optimal concentration range for hormone applications, requiring precise dilution for effectiveness.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Hormone concentration determines biological effect: too little produces no response; optimal concentration produces desired response; too much produces inhibitory or toxic effects. Understanding concentration-response relationships is essential for effective hormone applications in agriculture and horticulture.

worked_examples.title: Predicting Plant Height from Gibberellin Genotype
worked_examples.problem: Three wheat varieties have different GA signaling genotypes: Variety A (wild-type, normal GA sensitivity), Variety B (Rht mutation, DELLA protein resistant to degradation), Variety C (GA biosynthesis mutant, reduced GA production). All are grown under identical conditions. Predict: (1) relative plant heights, (2) which variety would benefit most from GA treatment, and (3) which variety is most likely to lodge (fall over) in high wind. Justify predictions.

step_by_step.explanation: Analyze Variety A (wild-type): normal GA production and signaling, normal height.
Analyze Variety B (Rht mutation): DELLAs resist degradation, suppressing growth even with normal GA.
Analyze Variety C (GA biosynthesis mutant): reduced GA production, less DELLA degradation.
Predict GA treatment response: Variety C can respond (has functional signaling); Variety B cannot (signaling blocked).
step_by_step.calculation: $\text{GA treatment: Variety C responds (taller); Variety B no response}$

step_by_step.explanation: Predict lodging risk: taller plants (Variety A) more prone to lodging than short plants (B, C).

worked_examples.final_answer: (1) Relative heights: A (tallest) > C (short) > B (shortest). (2) Variety C benefits most from GA treatment (restores GA levels); Variety B won't respond (signaling blocked). (3) Variety A most likely to lodge (tallest); B and C resistant to lodging (semi-dwarf/dwarf). This demonstrates how GA pathway mutations affect plant architecture and why Green Revolution varieties (like B) combined high yield with lodging resistance through modified GA signaling.
worked_examples.concept_applied: GA signaling genotype determines plant height and lodging resistance: biosynthesis mutations (low GA) can be rescued by GA treatment; signaling mutations (DELLA resistance) cannot. Understanding genetic basis of hormone responses enables informed variety selection and management decisions.

components.composition: Tryptophan aminotransferase (TAR), YUCCA flavin monooxygenases, indole-3-pyruvate decarboxylase; multiple pathways from tryptophan to IAA
components.function: Convert tryptophan to IAA through sequential enzymatic reactions; regulate auxin production rates in response to developmental and environmental signals
components.key_feature: YUCCA enzymes are rate-limiting and highly regulated; overexpression causes auxin overproduction phenotypes (epinasty, reduced apical dominance); mutation causes auxin deficiency (reduced growth, impaired tropisms)

components.composition: PIN efflux carriers (asymmetrically localized), AUX/LAX influx carriers, ABCB transporters; positioned on specific cell membrane faces
components.function: Mediate cell-to-cell auxin transport; establish concentration gradients through polar localization; redirect flow in response to environmental cues
components.key_feature: PIN protein localization is dynamic—repositioned by gravity, light, and developmental signals to redirect auxin flow; this plasticity enables tropic responses and developmental patterning

components.composition: Ent-kaurene synthase (plastids), ent-kaurene oxidase (ER), GA3ox and GA2ox (cytosol); multi-compartment pathway
components.function: Convert acetyl-CoA to active GAs through 10+ enzymatic steps; GA3ox activates inactive precursors; GA2ox deactivates active GAs
components.key_feature: GA3ox expression is environmentally regulated (light, temperature); GA2ox provides negative feedback; this two-enzyme system enables rapid GA level adjustment

components.composition: Auxin: TIR1/AFB receptors, Aux/IAA repressors, ARF transcription factors; GA: GID1 receptors, DELLA repressors, PIF/BZR transcription factors
components.function: Perceive hormone presence; transduce signal via protein degradation cascades; activate or repress target gene expression
components.key_feature: Both pathways use ubiquitin-proteasome degradation of repressors (Aux/IAA, DELLA); this provides rapid, reversible responses; different transcription factor combinations create tissue-specific responses

function.operation: Auxins and gibberellins function to coordinate plant growth and development by integrating environmental signals with internal developmental programs, enabling plants to optimize form and function for survival and reproduction.
function.energy: Hormone production and signaling require metabolic energy: auxin biosynthesis consumes tryptophan and ATP; polar transport requires ATP for PIN protein cycling; GA biosynthesis consumes acetyl-CoA and NADPH; signal transduction requires ATP for ubiquitination and proteasome function. Total cost is ~5-10% of plant energy budget, justified by growth optimization benefits.
function.mechanism: Hormone mechanisms integrate biosynthesis (production), transport (distribution), perception (reception), transduction (amplification), and response (growth). Environmental signals regulate each step: light affects biosynthesis genes; gravity affects transport protein localization; temperature affects receptor sensitivity. This multi-point regulation enables precise control of growth responses across diverse conditions.

cycle.operation: Enzymes convert precursors (tryptophan, acetyl-CoA) to active hormones (IAA, GA) in specific tissues
cycle.purpose: Produce hormones at sites where they're needed; regulate production rates in response to developmental and environmental signals

image.alt: Diagram showing auxin biosynthesis from tryptophan and gibberellin biosynthesis from acetyl-CoA with key enzymes labeled
image.caption: Hormone biosynthesis: auxin from tryptophan (left) via YUCCA enzymes; gibberellin from acetyl-CoA (right) via multi-step pathway in multiple cellular compartments.

cycle.operation: Auxin moves polarly via PIN proteins; gibberellins move non-polarly via vascular tissue; gradients established
cycle.purpose: Deliver hormones to target tissues; establish concentration gradients that pattern development; enable long-distance signaling

cycle.operation: Hormones bind receptors; repressor proteins degraded via ubiquitin-proteasome pathway; transcription factors activated
cycle.purpose: Convert hormone presence into gene expression changes; amplify signal through transcriptional cascades; enable rapid, reversible responses

cycle.operation: Target genes expressed; cellular processes modified (elongation, division, differentiation); hormone levels adjusted via feedback
cycle.purpose: Execute growth responses; maintain hormone homeostasis; coordinate with other hormones for integrated development

process_flow.description: Plant detects environmental cue (light, gravity, temperature, water) via photoreceptors, statocytes, thermosensors, or osmosensors.
process_flow.mechanism: Photoreceptors (phytochrome, cryptochrome) detect light quality/quantity; statoliths detect gravity orientation; temperature affects membrane fluidity and enzyme kinetics; water status affects turgor pressure.
process_flow.result: Signal transduction cascades initiated that regulate hormone biosynthesis, transport, or sensitivity genes.

process_flow.description: Environmental signals regulate expression of hormone biosynthesis enzymes, increasing or decreasing hormone production.
process_flow.mechanism: Transcription factors activated by environmental signals bind promoters of biosynthesis genes (YUCCA for auxin, GA3ox for GA); enzyme activity modulated by phosphorylation or allosteric regulation.
process_flow.result: Hormone levels increase or decrease in specific tissues, creating or modifying concentration gradients.

image.alt: Diagram showing environmental signals regulating hormone biosynthesis gene expression through transcription factors
image.caption: Biosynthesis regulation: environmental signals (light, gravity, temperature) activate transcription factors that regulate hormone biosynthesis genes.

process_flow.description: Hormones transported to target tissues; polar auxin transport creates concentration gradients; GA moves via vascular tissue.
process_flow.mechanism: PIN proteins repositioned to redirect auxin flow; vascular loading/unloading regulates GA distribution; concentration gradients established across tissues.
process_flow.result: Hormone concentration gradients pattern development: high auxin at shoot tip maintains apical dominance; GA gradients regulate stem elongation.

process_flow.description: Hormones bind receptors in target cells; repressor proteins degraded; transcription factors activated to regulate gene expression.
process_flow.mechanism: Auxin binds TIR1, degrading Aux/IAA; GA binds GID1, degrading DELLAs; freed transcription factors activate target genes.
process_flow.result: Gene expression changes initiate cellular responses: cell wall loosening, cell cycle activation, metabolic enzyme production.

image.alt: Flowchart showing hormone binding receptors, repressor degradation, transcription factor activation, and gene expression
image.caption: Signal transduction: hormone binds receptor (1), repressor degraded (2), transcription factor activated (3), target genes expressed (4).

process_flow.description: Target gene products modify cellular processes: cell wall enzymes loosen walls for elongation; cell cycle proteins promote division.
process_flow.mechanism: Expansins and XTH enzymes modify cell walls; cyclins and CDKs regulate cell cycle; metabolic enzymes provide building blocks and energy.
process_flow.result: Cells elongate, divide, or differentiate, producing visible growth responses: stem elongation, root branching, leaf expansion.

process_flow.description: Growth responses trigger feedback that adjusts hormone levels: biosynthesis genes downregulated; degradation enzymes upregulated.
process_flow.mechanism: Auxin induces Aux/IAA expression (negative feedback); GA induces GA2ox expression (deactivation); hormone levels return to baseline.
process_flow.result: Hormone homeostasis maintained; responses self-limiting; system reset for subsequent signals.

image.alt: Diagram showing negative feedback loops regulating hormone levels through biosynthesis and degradation gene expression
image.caption: Feedback regulation: hormone responses induce feedback genes that reduce hormone levels, maintaining homeostasis and preventing over-response.

process_types.energy_profile: Moderate energy cost: ATP for H⁺-ATPase pumping, auxin transport, and gene expression; rapid response (minutes) via acid growth, sustained response (hours) via gene expression
process_types.direction: Polar: auxin flows from shoot tip downward, creating gradient that patterns elongation along the axis
process_types.function: Enable rapid growth responses to environmental cues; pattern plant architecture through concentration gradients; coordinate elongation across tissues
goals: Maintain apical dominance for resource allocation
Initiate lateral organs at appropriate positions

primary_example.explanation: When light shines from one side, phototropins detect asymmetry and signal PIN proteins to reposition, redirecting auxin to the shaded side. Higher auxin on shaded side activates H⁺-ATPases, acidifying cell walls. Expansins activated, walls loosen, cells elongate faster on shaded side. Shoot bends toward light. Response occurs within 30 minutes, demonstrating rapid hormone-mediated environmental response. This maximizes photosynthetic efficiency by orienting leaves toward light.

image.alt: Diagram showing auxin redistribution to shaded side of shoot during phototropism with differential cell elongation
image.caption: Phototropism: light asymmetry (top) causes auxin redistribution to shaded side (middle), differential elongation bends shoot toward light (bottom).

examples.explanation: Root cap statocytes detect gravity via statolith sedimentation. Signal redirects auxin to lower side of root. High auxin inhibits elongation on lower side (opposite of shoots). Upper side elongates faster, root bends downward. This ensures roots grow into soil for water and nutrient acquisition.

Shoot tip produces auxin that flows down stem, inhibiting lateral bud growth through ethylene induction and cytokinin suppression. This maintains single dominant shoot, optimizing vertical growth for light competition. Pruning removes auxin source, releasing buds—basis for hedge trimming and tree shaping.

process_types.energy_profile: High energy cost: GA biosynthesis requires significant carbon and energy; cell elongation and division require ATP and building blocks; response over days to weeks
process_types.direction: Non-polar: GA moves bidirectionally through vascular tissue, enabling systemic signaling from production sites to target tissues
process_types.function: Enable rapid stem elongation for light competition; mobilize seed reserves for germination; coordinate flowering with environmental conditions

primary_example.explanation: Rosette plants (lettuce, Arabidopsis) grow with short stems until flowering signals (day length, temperature) increase GA biosynthesis. GA degrades DELLA repressors, releasing PIF transcription factors that activate cell wall loosening enzymes and cell cycle genes. Internodes elongate rapidly (up to 10 cm/day in some species), elevating flowers for pollination. This transition is irreversible—once bolted, plants commit to reproduction. Growers manipulate this with GA treatments (induce bolting for seed) or GA inhibitors (delay bolting for harvest).

image.alt: Time-lapse sequence showing rosette plant transitioning to bolted stem with GA-induced internode elongation
image.caption: Bolting transition: rosette (day 0) elongates stem (days 1-7) when GA increases, elevating flowers for pollination.

examples.explanation: Embryo produces GA that diffuses to aleurone layer. GA induces α-amylase and protease production, mobilizing starch and protein reserves. Sugars and amino acids fuel embryo growth until photosynthesis begins. This precise timing ensures reserves mobilized only when germination conditions are favorable. Barley brewers apply GA to malt, stimulating enzyme production for starch-to-sugar conversion.

Developing seeds produce GA that stimulates fruit cell division and elongation. Seedless grape varieties (lacking seed GA source) are treated with exogenous GA to achieve normal fruit size. GA treatment can increase berry size 50-100%, demonstrating commercial importance. GA coordinates with auxin (from developing seeds) and cytokinin (from fruit tissues) for optimal fruit development.

process_types.energy_profile: Variable energy cost: depends on which hormones dominate; balanced hormone ratios optimize resource allocation; imbalance wastes energy on inappropriate responses
process_types.direction: Integrated: multiple hormones act simultaneously on same tissues, with outcomes determined by relative concentrations and tissue sensitivity
process_types.function: Coordinate complex developmental processes requiring multiple hormonal inputs; integrate environmental and developmental signals; ensure robust responses through redundant pathways
goals: Ensure developmental stability despite environmental variation

primary_example.explanation: Plant tissue culture exploits auxin-cytokinin interactions: high auxin:cytokinin ratio induces roots; low ratio induces shoots; intermediate ratio produces undifferentiated callus. This demonstrates how hormone ratios, not absolute levels, determine developmental outcomes. The mechanism involves auxin promoting root-specific transcription factors (ARF7/19) while cytokinin promotes shoot-specific factors (WUS, STM). Commercial micropropagation uses this principle to mass-produce plants from small explants, enabling rapid multiplication of elite varieties.

image.alt: Diagram showing different auxin:cytokinin ratios producing roots, shoots, or callus in tissue culture
image.caption: Hormone ratio effects: high auxin (left) induces roots; high cytokinin (right) induces shoots; balanced (center) produces callus.

examples.explanation: GA promotes germination by degrading DELLAs; ABA promotes dormancy by stabilizing DELLAs. The GA:ABA ratio determines germination timing. Environmental cues shift the balance: light increases GA; drought increases ABA. This ensures seeds germinate only when conditions support seedling survival. Seed priming treatments manipulate this balance to enhance germination uniformity.

High auxin induces ethylene production, which inhibits root elongation. This creates negative feedback preventing excessive auxin accumulation. In waterlogged soils, ethylene accumulates (reduced gas exchange), inhibiting root growth—a survival response preventing root growth into anoxic conditions. Understanding this interaction explains why waterlogged plants show stunted roots despite adequate nutrients.

mechanisms.purpose: Auxin and gibberellin mechanisms enable plants to coordinate growth and development across their entire body without a central nervous system, integrating environmental signals with internal developmental programs to optimize survival and reproduction.

activation.definition: Hormone signaling is activated by environmental cues (light, gravity, temperature, water) and developmental signals (age, tissue identity) that regulate hormone biosynthesis, transport, or sensitivity.
activation.effect: Activation triggers growth responses appropriate to conditions: light promotes GA for stem elongation; shade increases auxin for phototropism; drought increases ABA to reduce growth. Mis-activation causes inappropriate responses: GA in darkness causes etiolation; auxin asymmetry without light causes spontaneous bending.

image.alt: Diagram showing environmental and developmental signals activating hormone biosynthesis, transport, and signaling pathways
image.caption: Hormone activation: environmental signals (light, gravity, temperature) and developmental signals (age, tissue type) regulate hormone pathways to coordinate growth with conditions.

key_properties.explanation: Hormone effects depend on concentration: low auxin promotes root growth; high auxin inhibits it. This enables one hormone to produce diverse effects in different tissues based on local concentration. Concentration is regulated by biosynthesis, transport, and degradation rates.

Auxin's polar transport creates directional gradients that pattern development; GA's non-polar transport enables systemic signaling. Transport mode determines hormone function: auxin patterns local development; GA coordinates whole-plant responses.

Both pathways use ubiquitin-proteasome degradation of repressors (Aux/IAA, DELLA), providing rapid, reversible responses. One hormone molecule can trigger degradation of many repressor molecules, amplifying the signal. This enables sensitive responses to low hormone concentrations.

Hormones interact through synergistic, antagonistic, and additive relationships. DELLA proteins interact with auxin, ABA, and ethylene signaling components, integrating multiple signals. This creates robust, tunable responses that account for multiple environmental factors simultaneously.

sections.relationship: Plant hormones (auxins and gibberellins) connect to previous sections: angiosperm reproduction (hormones regulate flowering, fruit development, seed germination); gymnosperm reproduction (GA stimulates pollen tube growth, seed development); pollination strategies (hormones regulate flower opening, nectar production, fruit set after pollination). Understanding hormone function is essential for agriculture (crop improvement, growth regulation), horticulture (propagation, pruning, flowering control), forestry (timber production, tree architecture), and ecology (plant-environment interactions, community dynamics). Hormones represent the molecular basis of plant plasticity—how the same genotype produces different phenotypes in different environments. This plasticity enables plants to optimize growth for survival and reproduction across diverse and changing conditions, explaining why plants dominate terrestrial ecosystems despite being sessile organisms.

summary: Auxins and gibberellins are master regulators of plant growth and development, functioning as chemical messengers that coordinate cellular responses to environmental and developmental signals. Auxins (primarily IAA) are produced in shoot tips and young leaves, transported polarly through PIN proteins, and regulate cell elongation, apical dominance, root initiation, and tropic responses through concentration-dependent effects and the TIR1-Aux/IAA-ARF signaling pathway. Gibberellins (primarily GA₁ and GA₃) are produced in young tissues and developing seeds, transported non-polarly through vascular tissue, and stimulate stem elongation, seed germination, flowering, and fruit development through the GID1-DELLA signaling pathway. Both hormones function at low concentrations (10⁻⁶ to 10⁻⁹ M) with effects determined by concentration, location, developmental stage, and interactions with other hormones. Key mechanisms include: polar auxin transport creating concentration gradients that pattern development; acid growth enabling rapid cell elongation; ubiquitin-proteasome degradation of repressors (Aux/IAA, DELLA) for rapid, reversible responses; and hormone crosstalk integrating multiple signals for coordinated growth. Environmental factors regulate hormone levels: light affects GA biosynthesis; gravity redirects auxin transport; temperature affects hormone sensitivity. Agricultural applications exploit hormone knowledge: synthetic auxins induce rooting in cuttings; GA treatments promote stem elongation and fruit size; GA biosynthesis inhibitors create semi-dwarf varieties resistant to lodging; hormone ratios control organ formation in tissue culture. Understanding auxin and gibberellin function reveals how plants achieve coordinated growth without a nervous system, how environmental factors influence development through hormonal regulation, and how agricultural practices can manipulate hormone levels to improve crop performance. These hormones exemplify plant plasticity—the ability to adjust growth to prevailing conditions—enabling plants to optimize survival and reproduction across diverse and changing environments.
